title: Warm Vent Watch
desc: Everrain survives because someone keeps track of how close the heat is getting.

steps.text:
Everrain is all water until it suddenly is not. Beneath the pools and behind the wet walls, heat still moves through the stone. Old timers in the cavern remember the breach that nearly turned this whole green hollow into steam and ruin, and they do not rely on memory alone to keep it from happening again.

Along the hotter shelves of the cavern, brass heat pins are driven into cracks where the rock thins toward old lava routes. Lower down, shallow runnels carry cool overflow across warm stone so workers can read the steam patterns and catch a change before it becomes a disaster. None of it is glamorous. All of it matters.

A vent watcher hands you a slate already spotted with water. "Pins or runnels," she says. "Either way you're learning why this place still exists."

options.label: Inspect the brass heat pins along the upper wall
Stir the cooling runnels and read the steam

steps.text:
The brass pins sit in neat rows where the wall changes from wet green stone to a darker mineral shine. Each is lacquered so its color shift reads clearly: dull gold for safe, amber for watch it, red for start shouting. You move with the vent watcher from pin to pin, wiping condensation away before comparing the metal against the color chart on her slate.

Most of the line stays reassuringly dull. One pin near a narrow crack has warmed to a deep yellow that is almost, but not quite, amber. The watcher marks it and waits a full minute before checking again. When the color settles back, she lets out a breath you had not realized she was holding.

"Good," she says, tapping the slate. "A mood, not a warning." In Everrain, that distinction is the difference between an ordinary afternoon and a cavern full of evacuation bells.

options.label: Finish the line and log the readings

steps.text:
The cooling runnels are shallow cuts in the stone that guide excess rainwater over the warmest shelves. Under normal conditions the steam rises in thin gray ribbons and drifts lazily toward the ceiling. When the heat underneath shifts, the ribbons curl differently - too fast, too tight, too angry.

You take a long rod and draw it gently through each runnel while the watcher studies the response. The water hisses, the steam lifts, and for a moment the whole ledge smells of mineral heat under clean rain. One channel gives off a thicker coil than the rest, enough to make the watcher crouch and stare hard before the pattern loosens and returns to normal.

She marks the spot for a second look at noon, then straightens with a grim little nod. "Nothing urgent. Which means the system worked." That is Everrain in one sentence: a place kept beautiful by creatures willing to monitor how close beauty always is to boiling.

options.label: Set the rod aside and keep the note
